**Abergavenny Town Hall** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Neuadd y Dref Y Fenni*) is a municipal building located on Cross Street, [Abergavenny](/wiki/Abergavenny "Abergavenny") in [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). The town hall, which also houses the town's market, is a Grade II [listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny_Town_Hall&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first building on the site was a market hall which had been financed by a legacy from the local [member of parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"), [Philip Jones](/wiki/Philip_Jones_(MP) "Philip Jones (MP)"), and which was completed in the early 17th century.[[2]](#cite_note-survey-2) Following their appointment in 1794,[[3]](#cite_note-hub-3) one of the first acts of the new Abergavenny Improvement Commissioners was to commission a new market hall which was designed by the well-known [Regency](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era") and [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") era architect, [John Nash](/wiki/John_Nash_(architect) "John Nash (architect)"), and completed in 1796.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the 1860s, in anticipation of a further increases in their responsibilities, including those of an urban sanitary authority, the commissioners decided to procure a town hall which would be built on the same site.[[2]](#cite_note-survey-2)

Construction of the new building, which was built with old red sandstone with Bath limestone and slate roofs, started in 1869.[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1) It was designed by [Wilson and Willcox](/wiki/James_Wilson_(architect) "James Wilson (architect)") of [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset") in the [Gothic Revival style](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture"), built by S. J. Moreland and Sons of [Gloucester](/wiki/Gloucester "Gloucester") and completed in 1871.[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1) The design involved an asymmetrical main frontage with five bays facing onto Cross Street; the central section featured an archway leading to a market hall flanked by two gothic windows on each side.[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1) There were five two-light [sash windows](/wiki/Sash_window "Sash window") divided by [Corinthian order](/wiki/Corinthian_order "Corinthian order") columns on the first floor and there was a clock tower with a copper roof at the north west corner.[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1) The building incorporated a corn exchange, municipal offices, a council chamber, a market hall, an assembly room and the local poor law offices.[[2]](#cite_note-survey-2) The assembly room was converted for use as a theatre in 1906 and subsequent performers there included the [rock band](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), who gave a concert in June 1963.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The building served as the headquarters of the Abergavenny Urban District Council for much of the 20th century but ceased to be local seat of government when the enlarged [Monmouth District Council](/wiki/District_of_Monmouth "District of Monmouth") was formed in 1974.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Abergavenny Town Council, which was formed in 1981,[[7]](#cite_note-7) made the building its home and installed two members of staff there.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Subsequent improvements included the modernisation of the theatre in 1996.[[1]](#cite_note-nhaw-1)

The cattle market, which had been held on the site for over 150 years, moved to [Bryngwyn](/wiki/Bryngwyn "Bryngwyn") in Monmouthshire in December 2013.[[9]](#cite_note-market-9) Other types of market continued to be held in the building, including a [flea market](/wiki/Flea_market "Flea market") which continued to be held there every Wednesday.[[9]](#cite_note-market-9) A programme of works, designed by GWP Architecture and costing £2.2 million, to convert the building into a community hub was completed in September 2020.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)
